items.title: Your Survival Guide to the Semiquincentennial Fourth of July Weekend
items.body:
America's 250th birthday lands on a Saturday, most workplaces are dark on Friday, and the travel industry has been bracing for one of the busiest holiday stretches ever recorded. Last year's Independence Day period drew a record **72 million-plus travelers**, and a once-in-a-generation anniversary is expected to push crowds even higher. Here's how to enjoy the weekend without spending it in a security line or a sea of brake lights.

## On the road

- **Drive at dawn.** The worst congestion on holiday weekends reliably builds from late morning through early evening. Leaving before 7 a.m. — or after 8 p.m. — can cut hours off a long trip.
- **Expect the return crush on Sunday afternoon.** With the holiday on Saturday, homebound traffic will concentrate on Sunday. If you can stretch the trip to Monday morning, do it.
- **Fuel and charge early.** Highway rest stops and fast chargers nearest major metros will be slammed; top off before you leave town.

## At the airport

- Arrive **at least two hours early** for domestic flights — three for international — and book the first departure of the day, which is statistically the least likely to be delayed.
- Summer afternoon thunderstorms are the top cause of holiday delays. A morning flight isn't just less crowded; it's better weather insurance.

## Catching the show

The marquee celebrations — Philadelphia's anniversary festivities, the fireworks over the National Mall, Macy's display in New York, the Boston Pops on the Esplanade — will be historic, and historically crowded. A few sanity-savers:

- **Go local.** Nearly every county fairground and minor-league ballpark in America has its own show. The 250th will be celebrated everywhere, not just on TV.
- **Stake out water views.** Fireworks over a river or harbor double the spectacle and spread the crowd along miles of shoreline.
- **Mind the heat.** Early July is peak heat-advisory season. Water, shade, and sunscreen are as essential as the picnic blanket.

## One more idea

If crowds aren't your thing, consider the counter-programming move: national parks and small historic towns on the holiday itself, then the big-city energy on Sunday when everyone else is driving home. However you spend it, this is the Fourth people will be talking about for the next 25 years — pace yourself accordingly.
